content.full_text:
EDD Serving the People of California

State of California / Health and Welfare Agency

Pete Wilson, Governor

September 11, 1991

55:113:Hallabrin:pdr
(916) 445-7656

Scott Hallabrin, Acting General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
PO Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Mr. Hallabrin:

REQUEST FOR WRITTEN ADVICE

The Legal Office at the Employment Development Department
("Department") requests your written advice concerning
application of the conflict of interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act ("Act") to the circumstances set forth in
this memorandum.

An individual is a member of the State Job Training
Coordinating Council ("Council"), an advisory body to the
Governor for the implementation of the Job Training
Partnership Act. (29 U.S.C.A. secs. 1501-1781.) Council
members are appointed by the Governor pursuant to the
provisions of 29 U.S.C.A. section 1532. The purpose of the
Job Training Partnership Act is " . . . to prepare youth and
unskilled adults for entry into the labor force and to afford
job training to those economically disadvantaged individuals
and other individuals facing serious barriers to employment,
who are in special need of such training to obtain productive
employment." (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1501.) Funds from the federal
government support job training services administered at the
local level through service delivery areas. The Council
advises the Governor about the designation of service delivery
areas. The Council's duties also include advising the
Governor and local entities on job training plans. Seventy-
eight percent of the funds for the service delivery areas
within a state are allotted each fiscal year according to a

Employment Development Department / P.O. Box 826880 / Sacramento CA 94280-0001

Scott Hallabrin                    -2-                 September 11, 1991


formula.  (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1602(a)(1).)  Six percent of a
State's allotment for each fiscal year is used for incentive
grants for service delivery areas that exceed certain
performance standards. (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1602(b)(3)(B).)  One
of the functions of the Council is to recommend variations in
performance standards.  (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1532(b)(2).)

The Council sets criteria for the granting of the Title III
discretionary funds for employment and training assistance for
dislocated workers.  A Review Committee comprised of Council
member(s), JTPA representatives and representatives from other
state agencies evaluates the proposals for funding from
service delivery areas and makes a recommendation to the
Director of EDD who then makes the final decision.  A Council
member participates in a panel that ranks requests for money
disbursed to veterans. (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1721.)

The Council member who is seeking advice is an officer, board
member and 18-20% owner of a business (the company) that
provides engineering, architectural services and construction
management to public agencies in California.  The company has
two locations, one in Concord, California and one in Los
Alamitos, California.  The company leases the real property on
which the business operates.  Most of the public agencies are
cities; however, the biggest client is the Department of
Transportation (Cal Trans).  The company often contracts with
Cal Trans to provide inspectors who monitor quality control on
Cal Trans construction projects.  Contracts with cities
frequently provide inspection of and construction management
for public facilities.  The City of Anaheim, which is a
service delivery area, is one of the company's clients.  The
company designs parks for the City of Anaheim.  Most of the
company's contracts are obtained through competitive process.
The company does not perform any job training for service
delivery areas.

To the extent that the Council member votes on the granting of
money (see earlier discussion of JTPA fund allocation) to a
private industry council which oversees the job training plan
for a service delivery area (20 U.S.C.A. sec. 1503), then
clients of the company within the service delivery area
benefit.  The question arises whether the Council member whose
company then contracts with public agencies benefited from the
Job Training Partnership Act (JTPA) money that a service
delivery area receives.  It is important to note that JTPA
money cannot, by statute, displace funds used for other
purposes.  "Funds provided under this Act shall only be used

Scott Hallabrin                -3-                September 11, 1991

for activities which are in addition to those which would
otherwise be available in the area in the absence of such
funds." (29 U.S.C.A. sec. 1551(a).)

Please contact me if you need more information.

Sincerely,

[handwritten signature: Vera Sandronsky]
VERA SANDRONSKY
Staff Counsel
